parentQuestion: Dark adaptation involves accumulation of rhodopsin.

scaffolds.q: Rhodopsin is the photopigment in ROD photoreceptors.
In bright light, rhodopsin gets 'bleached' (broken down by light).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Photon absorption isomerizes 11-cis-retinal to all-trans-retinal.

scaffolds.q: More bleaching = less rhodopsin available = lower rod sensitivity.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Fewer photon 'catchers' = less ability to detect dim light.

scaffolds.q: In darkness, bleaching STOPS but regeneration CONTINUES.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The RPE converts all-trans-retinal back to 11-cis-retinal.

scaffolds.q: As rhodopsin accumulates, rod sensitivity INCREASES.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! More rhodopsin = more photon catchers = better night vision.

scaffolds.q: Full dark adaptation takes 20-40 minutes (slow rhodopsin regeneration).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! That's why it takes time to see well in a dark theater.

scaffolds.q: Dark adaptation = rhodopsin accumulation = restored rod sensitivity.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The molecular basis of adjusting to darkness.

scaffolds.q: Dark adaptation requires rhodopsin regeneration and accumulation.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! RPE rebuilds 11-cis-retinal, which recombines with opsin to form rhodopsin.

scaffolds.q: The answer is TRUE - dark adaptation involves rhodopsin accumulation.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! In darkness, bleaching stops while regeneration continues. Net effect: Rhodopsin builds up, sensitivity increases, you can eventually detect single photons.
